title: Colossians 3
subtitle: The New Self and the Christian Household

sections.header: Verses 1–11 — "Set Your Minds on Things Above"

heb.gloss: set your minds on things above
heb.paragraph: The imperative phroneite (v. 2) commands a sustained orientation of thought toward the heavenly realities where Christ is seated. This is not escapism but eschatological realism: believers’ true life is ‘hidden with Christ in God’ (v. 3), and when Christ appears, they will appear with him in glory (v. 4). The heavenly orientation provides the perspective from which earthly conduct is reshaped.

The put-off/put-on framework of vv. 9–10 parallels Eph 4:22–24. The old self (palaion anthrōpon) with its practices has been stripped off; the new self (ton neon) is being renewed in knowledge in the image of its Creator. The renewal language echoes Gen 1:26–27: the new creation restores the image of God that was marred by sin.

refs.note: The parallel put-off/put-on instruction in Ephesians uses nearly identical language, confirming the baptismal-catechetical origin of the framework.
Paul’s argument that death with Christ means death to sin’s dominion provides the theological basis for Col 3:1–5: those who have been raised with Christ must live accordingly.
The dissolution of ethnic, social, and gender categories ‘in Christ’ parallels Col 3:11, though Colossians adds ‘barbarian, Scythian’ to the list, reflecting the multi-cultural context.

notes.note: The heavenly orientation is grounded in the believer’s union with the risen Christ. MacArthur notes that ‘your life is now hidden with Christ in God’ (v. 3) expresses the security of the believer’s position: hidden from the world’s view but secure in God’s keeping. The ‘appearing’ (v. 4) is the parousia, when the hidden reality becomes manifest.

The vice list is not merely prohibitive but mortificatory: ‘put to death’ (nekrōsate, v. 5) demands decisive action against sin. MacArthur distinguishes the positional (already dead with Christ, v. 3) from the practical (must actively kill sinful habits, v. 5). The dissolution of ethnic and social barriers in v. 11 is the practical consequence of the new creation.

Calvin reads the heavenly orientation as the foundation of all Christian ethics: right action flows from right perspective. Setting the mind on things above does not mean withdrawal from earthly responsibility but evaluating earthly matters from a heavenly vantage point.

Calvin notes the violence of the metaphor: ‘put to death’ does not mean reform or moderate but kill. Sin is not to be negotiated with but executed. The renewal of the new self is continuous (anakainoumenon, present participle): sanctification is an ongoing process.

The conditional ei oun synēgerthēte (if then you have been raised) uses the indicative, presenting the condition as factual: ‘since you have been raised.’ The aorist passive synēgerthēte points back to baptism (2:12) as the moment of resurrection with Christ.

The list extends Gal 3:28 by adding ‘barbarian, Scythian’—the Scythians being regarded as the most uncivilised people in the ancient world. The point: even the most extreme cultural divide is overcome in Christ.

Moo reads the section as the ethical counterpart to the christological fullness of chs. 1–2. If believers are complete in Christ (2:10), then the old patterns of behaviour are incompatible with their new identity. The already-not yet framework governs: believers are already raised (v. 1) but must still put to death (v. 5) and be renewed (v. 10).

O’Brien identifies the passage as structured by the put-off/put-on framework with the heavenly-orientation motif as its grounding. The imperative to seek things above (v. 1) and think about things above (v. 2) establishes the eschatological perspective that governs the entire ethical section. O’Brien notes that the renewal ‘in knowledge in the image of its Creator’ (v. 10) recalls the creation narrative: the new self restores what was lost in the fall.

hist.context: Colossians 3 marks the transition from polemic (ch. 2) to paraenesis (chs. 3–4). The opening ‘since, then, you have been raised with Christ’ (v. 1) connects the ethical instruction to the christological foundation: the believers’ resurrection with Christ (2:12) has consequences for daily life. Paul commands them to ‘set your hearts on things above’ (v. 1) and ‘set your minds on things above, not on earthly things’ (v. 2). The vice list (vv. 5–9) catalogues behaviours that belong to the old self: sexual immorality, impurity, lust, evil desires, greed, anger, rage, malice, slander, filthy language, lying. The declaration of v. 11 echoes Gal 3:28: ‘Here there is no Gentile or Jew, circumcised or uncircumcised, barbarian, Scythian, slave or free, but Christ is all, and is in all.’

sections.header: Verses 12–17 — "Clothe Yourselves with Compassion, Kindness, and Love"

heb.gloss: to put on, clothe oneself with
heb.paragraph: The verb endysasthe (v. 12) continues the clothing metaphor: having stripped off the old self (v. 9), believers must now ‘clothe yourselves with’ a new set of virtues. The five virtues listed—compassion, kindness, humility, gentleness, patience—are all community-building dispositions. Over all of them, love is the ‘perfect bond of unity’ (v. 14).

refs.note: The parallel forgiveness imperative in Ephesians—‘be kind and compassionate, forgiving each other, just as in Christ God forgave you’—uses the same christological basis as Col 3:13.
The parallel exhortation to mutual edification through psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs with thanksgiving corresponds closely to Col 3:16–17.

notes.note: The election language (‘God’s chosen people, holy and dearly loved’) grounds the ethical call in identity: you behave this way because of who you are. Love is the ‘perfect bond of unity’—the garment that holds all other virtues together. Forgiveness is ‘as the Lord forgave you’—the christological pattern is the standard.

The peace of Christ as ‘umpire’ is a vivid image: when conflict arises, Christ’s peace adjudicates. The word of Christ dwelling richly produces a community that teaches, admonishes, and worships. The comprehensive principle of v. 17—everything in the name of Jesus—extends Christian worship into every sphere of life.

Calvin reads love as the summation of all virtues: without it, the others are isolated achievements rather than an integrated character. Forgiveness is not merely recommended but commanded, and the standard is divine: as the Lord forgave you. Calvin notes that this standard is simultaneously humbling and liberating.

Calvin emphasises that the word of Christ must dwell ‘richly’ (plousiōs)—not sparingly or superficially. Mutual teaching and admonition are the responsibility of the whole community, not merely of its leaders.

The phrase syndesmos tēs teleiotētos (bond of perfection/unity) is debated: does love bind the community together, or does love bind the virtues together? The NET favours the former: love is the bond that perfects the community’s unity.

The phrase ho logos tou Christou (the word of Christ) is unique—the only NT occurrence. It may refer to Christ’s teaching, the gospel message about Christ, or the message that Christ himself speaks through the community.

Moo reads the virtue catalogue as the positive expression of the new self’s character. The christological basis of forgiveness (v. 13) and the Christ-centred summary (v. 17) ensure that the ethical programme is not generic moralism but specifically Christian: every virtue is rooted in and oriented toward Christ.

O’Brien identifies three organising principles: identity (‘God’s chosen people,’ v. 12), love (‘over all these virtues,’ v. 14), and the word of Christ (‘dwelling richly,’ v. 16). Together they produce a community characterised by mutual care, forgiveness, and worship—the visible evidence of the new creation announced in v. 10.

hist.context: The virtue catalogue (vv. 12–14) is the positive counterpart to the vice list of vv. 5–9. Paul addresses the community as ‘God’s chosen people, holy and dearly loved’ (v. 12)—election language that grounds ethical imperatives in divine identity. The five virtues (compassion, kindness, humility, gentleness, patience) are supplemented by mutual forbearance and forgiveness (v. 13), with love as the crowning garment that ‘binds them all together in perfect unity’ (v. 14). The peace of Christ is to rule (brabeueō, literally ‘to act as umpire’) in their hearts (v. 15). The word of Christ is to dwell in them richly as they teach and admonish one another through psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs (v. 16). The summary principle: ‘whatever you do, whether in word or deed, do it all in the name of the Lord Jesus, giving thanks to God the Father through him’ (v. 17).

heb.gloss: to submit, place under, be subject to
heb.paragraph: The verb hypotassesthe (v. 18) is the same term used in the Ephesian household code (Eph 5:22). In Colossians, the instruction is briefer but carries the same christological qualifier: submission is ‘as is fitting in the Lord.’ The Lord’s lordship is the framework within which all household relationships are negotiated.

refs.note: The Ephesian household code provides the fuller parallel, with extensive theological rationale (especially for the husband-wife relationship). Colossians offers the concise version.
Peter’s household code addresses slaves, wives, and husbands with similar christological framing, though Peter emphasises suffering as a dimension of the slaves’ calling.

notes.note: The wife’s submission is qualified: ‘as is fitting in the Lord.’ The husband’s love is also commanded: ‘do not be harsh with them.’ MacArthur notes the balance: submission without love is oppression; love without order is chaos.

Children’s obedience ‘pleases the Lord’; fathers must not ‘embitter’ their children. MacArthur notes that parental authority is not unlimited but bounded by the child’s welfare: provocation that discourages or crushes violates the command.

The slave instructions reframe all work as service to Christ. The motivation is eschatological: ‘you will receive an inheritance from the Lord as a reward’ (v. 24). MacArthur notes the subversive logic: slaves, who had no legal right to inheritance, receive the ultimate inheritance from the Lord they truly serve.

Calvin reads the slave instructions as applicable to all who work under authority. The principle is universal: whatever one’s earthly station, the ultimate employer is Christ. Calvin notes the justice principle of v. 25: ‘anyone who does wrong will be repaid for their wrongs, and there is no favouritism’—this applies to masters as well as slaves.

The phrase hōs anēken en kyriō (as is fitting in the Lord) uses the imperfect tense of anēkō, indicating what is continuously appropriate. The NET notes that ‘in the Lord’ provides the sphere within which fitness is determined.

The clause tō kyriō Christō douleuete (you are serving the Lord Christ) may be indicative (statement of fact) or imperative (command). The NET translators favour the imperative: serve the Lord Christ!

Moo reads the household code as the concrete application of the general principle of 3:17: doing everything in the name of the Lord Jesus. Each relationship—marriage, parenting, work—is transformed by the recognition that Christ is the ultimate authority. Moo notes the pastoral connection to Philemon: the slave-master instructions are addressed to the same community that will receive Onesimus back.

O’Brien notes the christological concentration: every instruction is grounded in the Lord’s authority, the Lord’s pleasure, or the Lord’s reward. The household code is not a concession to culture but a demonstration of how Christ’s lordship reshapes the most fundamental social relationships from within.

hist.context: The Colossian household code (Haustafel) addresses three pairs of relationships: wives and husbands (vv. 18–19), children and fathers (vv. 20–21), slaves and masters (vv. 22–25, continuing into 4:1). The code is briefer than Ephesians’ version but shares the same christological transformation: each relationship is reframed by the phrase ‘in the Lord’ (vv. 18, 20) or ‘as working for the Lord’ (v. 23). The slave-master section is the longest, reflecting the pastoral urgency of the Onesimus situation (cf. Philemon). Slaves are instructed to work ‘with all your heart, as working for the Lord, not for human masters’ (v. 23), with the assurance that ‘it is the Lord Christ you are serving’ (v. 24).

themes.note: Holiness dominates as the chapter provides comprehensive ethical instruction. Worship is prominent through the singing and thanksgiving instructions. Sovereignty undergirds the household code: Christ is Lord over every relationship.

rows.label: vv.1–4
rows.text: Set your minds above: raised with Christ, life hidden in God

rows.label: vv.5–9
rows.text: Put to death: vice list; strip off the old self

rows.label: vv.10–11
rows.text: Put on the new self: renewed in the Creator’s image; no barriers

rows.label: vv.12–14
rows.text: Clothe yourselves: compassion, kindness, love the perfect bond

rows.label: vv.15–17
rows.text: Peace as umpire; word of Christ; everything in Jesus’ name

rows.label: vv.22–25
rows.text: Slaves: work for the Lord Christ; the inheritance as reward

lit.note: Colossians 3 transitions from christological polemic to ethical instruction, structured by the put-off/put-on framework (vv. 1–11), the virtue catalogue (vv. 12–17), and the household code (vv. 18–25). The governing principle: since believers are raised with Christ, every dimension of life must reflect the new creation.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads The New Self and the Christian Household within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

discourse.thesis: Since you were raised with Christ, put on the new self — let the peace of Christ rule and the word of Christ dwell richly.

nodes.text: If you were raised with Christ, seek the things above where Christ is seated. Your life is hidden with Christ in God. When Christ appears, you will appear with him in glory.

Put to death what is earthly: sexual immorality, passion, evil desire, covetousness, anger, malice, slander, obscene talk, lying. You have put off the old self and put on the new self — there is no Greek and Jew, barbarian, Scythian, slave, free; Christ is all.

As God's chosen, put on compassion, kindness, humility, meekness, patience. Bear with one another; forgive as the Lord forgave. Above all, put on love. Let Christ's peace rule, his word dwell richly. Do everything in the name of the Lord Jesus.

Wives, submit to husbands. Husbands, love your wives. Children, obey parents. Fathers, do not provoke children. Bondservants, obey in everything — work heartily as for the Lord. Masters, treat bondservants justly.

coaching.tip: 'Set your minds on things above' (v. 2). Not escapism—the 'above' relocates the self. 'Your life is now hidden with Christ in God' (v. 3). Identity is secured in heavenly places; earthly behavior flows from that security. You are already there; live accordingly.

'Clothe yourselves with compassion, kindness, humility, gentleness and patience' (v. 12). After stripping off vices (vv. 5-9), believers dress in virtues. The wardrobe is Christ himself: 'over all these virtues put on love' (v. 14). Character is chosen, not merely felt.
